coredata.dc:title: Local photothermal/photodynamic synergistic antibacterial therapy based on two-dimensional BP@CQDs triggered by single NIR light source
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Baolei
Su, Yutian
Wu, Shishan
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic bacteria-infected wound healing faces challenges even though many advanced antibiotics and antibacterial nanoagents have been developed. Herein, we established a two-dimensional antibacterial nanoplatform with synergistic photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) antibacterial capabilities mediated by a single 808 nm laser irradiation. The nanoplatform is constructed by combining black phosphorus (BP) obtained by liquid phase exfoliation and hydrothermally prepared tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared by electrostatic interaction. As a result, the photothermal conversion of BP and hydroxyl radical (‧OH) production of CQDs under NIR laser makes the nanoplatform (BP@CQDs) possess an outstanding antibacterial performance against S. aureus and E. coli (as high as 92.7% and 98.4%, respectively), resulting in a faster wound closure ratio than another infected wound. Moreover, in vitro and in vivo researches showed that BP@CQDs have good hemocompatibility, cytocompatibility, and biocompatibility during the therapeutic process. This work demonstrates the broad application prospect of BP nanosheets in infectious microenvironments and develops a potential strategy for S. aureus-infected wound repair.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black phosphorus
Carbon quantum dots
Photothermal and photodynamic
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Te-doped CQDs and BP@CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation of Te-doped CQDs. (B) TEM image of BPs. (C) TEM image of Te-doped CQDs. (D) TEM image of BP@CQDs (Scale bar=30 µm). E) XPS spectrum of BP@CQDs.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of BP@CQDs. (B) Photothermal curves and (C) Infrared thermal images of BP@CQDs with NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) and the concentration from left to right is 0, 25, 50, 100 µg/mL. D) ESR signal of CQDs, CQDs and BP@CQDs under 808 nm laser using DEMP as spin-trapping agent. E) Intracellular ROS in S. aureus and E. coli monitored by DCFH-DA after different treatments (Scale bar: 30 µm).

(A) Digital photos and (B) hemolysis assay of red blood cells (RBCs) incubated with different concentrations of BP@CQDs (Scale bar: 50 µm). (C) Fluorescence images by calcein AM/ propidium iodide (PI) staining of L929 cells after incubation in BP@CQDs solutions with different concentrations for 1 day (Scale bar: 30 µm). (D) Cell viability of L929 cells incubated with different concentrations of BP@CQDs for 24 h.

(A) In vitro antibacterial assay showing with bacteria clones of BP@CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus. The corresponding bacterial viability evaluated by counting amount of (B) E. coli and (C) S. aureus. Thermal photos of mice with NIR irradiation and the relative photothermal curves. (D, E) Thermal images of mice under NIR irradiation and the corresponding photothermal heating curves.

(A) Photographs of mice traumas by different treatments. (B) Relative wound area of mice traumas by different treatments. (C) Body weight of mice in different groups. (D) Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining of reconstructed skin tissues (Scale bar: 100 µm).

head.ce:title: Local photothermal/photodynamic synergistic antibacterial therapy based on two-dimensional BP@CQDs triggered by single NIR light source

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, 163 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, 163 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing 210023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic bacteria-infected wound healing faces challenges even though many advanced antibiotics and antibacterial nanoagents have been developed. Herein, we established a two-dimensional antibacterial nanoplatform with synergistic photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) antibacterial capabilities mediated by a single 808 nm laser irradiation. The nanoplatform is constructed by combining black phosphorus (BP) obtained by liquid phase exfoliation and hydrothermally prepared tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared by electrostatic interaction. As a result, the photothermal conversion of BP and hydroxyl radical (‧OH) production of CQDs under NIR laser makes the nanoplatform (BP@CQDs) possess an outstanding antibacterial performance against S. aureus and E. coli (as high as 92.7% and 98.4%, respectively), resulting in a faster wound closure ratio than another infected wound. Moreover, in vitro and in vivo researches showed that BP@CQDs have good hemocompatibility, cytocompatibility, and biocompatibility during the therapeutic process. This work demonstrates the broad application prospect of BP nanosheets in infectious microenvironments and develops a potential strategy for S. aureus-infected wound repair.

Black phosphorus loaded with Te-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have synergistic photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) antibacterial capabilities mediated by a single 808 nm laser source contain
Image, graphical abstract
.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is one of the main threats to human life [1]. A variety of antibiotic agents, such as quinolones, β-lactam, macrocyclic lactones, and aminoglycoside antibiotics, were developed and achieved great success in fighting pathogenic bacteria [2–5]. However, the misuse of antibiotics has also given rise the drug-resistant bacteria, leading to many serious side effects in the clinic. Although antibiotics towards "super bacteria" have been discovered, bacterial resistance has continuously increased and may outpace the development of antibiotics [6,7]. Several new antimicrobial strategies have been designed to address these problems, including utilizing metal ions and metallic oxide to destroy DNA and cell walls of bacteria. However, their toxic effects on human health are controversial [8–10]. Therefore, it is significative to develop new efficient and safe antimicrobial drugs or approaches towards drug-resistant bacteria.

Black phosphorus (BP) is a new member of the two-dimensional nanomaterials family and has received enormous interest because of its unique honeycomb network, n-plane anisotropy, and photocatalytic properties [11–13]. In particular, the thickness-dependent bandgap (0.3–2.0 eV) of BP nanosheets endows them excellent photothermal and photodynamic properties at 650 nm and 808 nm laser, respectively [14–16]. BP also has good biocompatibility and a single safe component of phosphorus, which can be a candidate in biomedical applications [17]. Our latest work found that BP combined with AuNPs can provide a synergistic antimicrobial effect on bacteria under a single 650 nm laser, which provides photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) capability with one laser source [18]. Currently, photothermal therapy and photodynamic therapy have been developed greatly, and many researchers have conducted in-depth research on them, and also found that the synergistic effect of photothermal therapy and photodynamic therapy can better kill the bacteria [19,20]. Ma et al. prepared a nanocomposite containing methylene blue, graphene oxide and pluronic F127, which has both good photothermal and photodynamic properties and achieved good therapeutic effects [21]. However, the tissue penetration and phototoxicity of 650 nm laser make it unsuitable for clinical usage. As a result, using NIR light with strong penetration and few side effects to stimulate BP-based nanoplatform to play two therapeutic effects is more promising. Besides, it may be possible to fabricate non-toxic and versatile antibacterial nanohybrids by taking advantage of its harmless degradation products (phosphite and phosphate) and strong binding affinities between metal and phosphorus atoms.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel kind of carbon-based zero-dimensional material, have attracted much attention in biological applications due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, a good deal of raw materials, good water solubility, and biocompatibility [22]. Recently, a broad series of strategies have been developed to improve the physicochemical properties of CQDs through doping heteroatoms, such as S, N, Se [23–25]. Compared with pure CQDs, these heteroatoms-dope CQDs could achieve extra features by utilizing heteroatoms to change the original structure of CQDs or serve as reactive sites. For example, Han et al. doped duplex metal (Ag&Cu) into CQDs with L-arginine as a precursor, resulting in further DOX-loading/releasing and synergistic tumor theranostics [26]. Travlou et al. systematically studied the bactericidal activity of S- and N-doped CQDs against representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains, finding that the bactericidal capability was linked to their specific nanoscale surface chemistry and sizes, as well as possible electrostatic interactions and activate oxygen species [27]. Chen et al. used the tellurium (Te)-containing CQDs for scavenging H2O2 and generating hydroxyl radical (‧OH) under 808 nm irradiation this suggests that heteroatom-doped CQDs can generate free radicals as near-infrared photodynamic therapeutic agents under NIR [28].

Herein, a NIR-activated antibacterial nanoplatform consisting of BP and Te-doped CQDs (BPs@CQDs) was designed and fabricated to achieved synergetic antibacterial activity. The developed BPs@CQDs can perform PTT/PDT dual-mode therapy against bacterial infections under a single wavelength. BP nanosheets were first prepared from bulk BP by liquid-phase exfoliation like other 2D materials [29]. Then, Te-doped CQDs were synthesized using a simple and efficient hydrothermal method. Finally, the prepared Te-doped CQDs are grafted onto BP nanosheets' surface via physical interactions, mainly by electrostatic interaction (PO4 3− on BP provides a negative charge [30], and Te/NH2 on CQDs offers a positive charge). The obtained BPs@CQDs bactericidal nanoplatform possess good biocompatibility, excellent photothermal conversion efficiency, and ‧OH production capacity under NIR irradiation. When irradiated with 808 nm laser, the BP nanosheet could generate much heat for bacteria ablation and produce enough ‧OH to oxidize and destroy bacteria's cell wall, thus achieved NIR activated PTT/PDT synergistic therapy. The antibacterial activities and biocompatibility of the as-prepared BPs@CQDs nanoplatform have been demonstrated. Our BPs@CQDs provide a promising candidate of the multimodal nano-antibacterial platform for the combined treatment of pathogenic bacteria and repairing infected wounds.

The bulk BP was obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. The β-Chloro-L-alanine was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. PBS was obtained from Thermo-Fisher (USA). 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 2,7-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), NaOH, and Na2Te2 were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

BP nanosheet was prepared according to the traditional liquid phase stripping method. Bulk BP crystal was crushed into powder without oxygen and then ultrasonic stripped by NMP solution. Briefly, 10 mg of BP powder was added into 20 mL NaOH saturated NMP solution. After the complete mixture, an ice bath ultrasound was used to strip the BP nanosheet for 6 h. The BP suspension was then separated via low-speed centrifugation to obtain a single or few nanosheets. Finally, the supernatant obtained by centrifugation was treated with high-speed centrifugation to remove the NMP solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Te-doped CQDs and BP@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Te-doped-CQDs were synthesized according to a previously reported literature from tellurocystine precursor, obtained from β-Chloro-L-alanine [31]. Then, 10 mg of tellurocystine was dissolved into 10 mL of deionized water, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 9 for good solubility. The weakly alkaline solution was then transferred to a round-bottomed flask and heated to 60 °C for 24 h. After the end of the reaction, the resulting brown was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 r.min−1 for 10 min. Eventually, the supernatant was collected and washed with the dialysis membrane (Mw=600 Da) to obtain Te-doped CQDs. The BP@CQDs were prepared using a simple mixing method. Briefly, the BP nanosheet was added into Te-doped CQDs solution, and the suspension was stirred overnight. The products were collected by centrifugation and washed with deionized water several times. The characterization details of BPs@AuNPs nanocomposites were added in the supplementary materials.

The mature synthetic method of β-Chloro-L-alanine was reported by Satheeshkumar et al., which has a good yield from L-serine and could be purchased directly [31]. Then, the β-Chloro-L-alanine was used as a precursor to synthesize tellurocystine as the carbon resource of Te-doped CQDs. After a simple and facile hydrothermal reaction, the CQDs with Te atoms and amino groups on its surface were obtained (Fig. 1 A). The typical microstructure and morphologies of BP nanosheets, Te-doped CQDs, and BP@CQDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1B, BP nanosheets present a smooth lamellar structure with the size of 100–150 nm, and a partial sheet-like overlap was also observed on the BP nanosheets. The structure of BP nanosheets was further confirmed with the Raman spectrum (Fig. S1). Three typical characteristic peaks of BP at 356 cm−1, 434 cm−1, and 462 cm−1 could be observed from BP powder and nanosheets, corresponding to the B2g, Ag2 in-plane symmetrical vibration, and Ag1 out-of-plane symmetrical vibration of BP [32]. The similar Raman spectra from BP powder and nanosheets also demonstrated the retention of the structure and basic properties of BP nanosheets under intense ultrasound. And we also used xrd to probe the changes of black phosphorus nanosheets before and after loading quantum dots, as shown in Fig. S2, the peak intensity of black phosphorus nanosheets after loading quantum dots appeared to be significantly reduced. A weak peak at 41.4° and 44° might be due to the presence of Te-doped CQDs. TEM image of Te-doped CQDs was collected in Fig. 1C. A typical spherical shape with a 1–10 nm diameter and good dispersion can be seen, indicating that the CQDs were successfully prepared and have good water solubility. Furthermore, the TEM of BP@CQDs in Fig. 1D demonstrated that Te-doped CQDs in the form of nanodots are distributed randomly on the surface of BP nanosheets. The corresponding X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in Figs. 1E and S3 showed the existence of various characteristic elements from the typical peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, P 2p, and Te 3d, thus indicating the formation of nanoplatform that BP nanosheets covered with Te-doped CQDs [33]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of BP@CQDs showed the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 doublets at 129.2 and 130.1 eV, respectively, which were characteristic peaks of crystalline BP. The peak located at 134 eV may be some free oxidized phosphorus elements. Meanwhile, the XPS of Te shows that its valence state remains essentially unchanged (Fig. S4). These results strongly confirm that the BP@CQDs nanoplatform was successfully prepared by introducing Te-doped CQDs onto the BP surface and via electrostatic interactions.

In order to study the photothermal property of BP@CQDs, we first measure the light absorption capacity of BP@CQDs in the near-infrared region with UV-vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a strong NIR absorption at 808 nm, indicating that BP@CQDs could absorb NIR and perform the photothermal conversion. The further photothermal performance of BP@CQDs during NIR light irradiation at the density of 1.5 W/cm2 was shown in Fig. 2B. The temperatures of all the concentration samples displayed an evident increase rapidly and reached a plateau in 5 min, and even reached up to 23.8 to 50.6 °C with the highest concentration of 100 µg/mL, suggesting that BP@CQDs could cause severe thermal damage on pathogenic bacteria. Meanwhile, the solution temperature experiments and photothermal stability experiments at different laser powers proved that the BP@CQDs have good photothermal properties and stability (Figs. S5 and 6). After calculation, the photothermal conversion efficiency of BP@CQDs reached at 21.3%. Besids, BP@CQDs exhibited a concentration-dependent photothermal effect. A higher concentration of samples leads to a higher temperature under the NIR irradiation because BP plays the role of absorbing NIR energy in the nanoplatform. The corresponding infrared thermal images of BP@CQDs in Fig. 2C also proved the potential of BP@CQDs as a photothermal agent.

The reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation efficiency of BP@CQDs under NIR irradiation was first investigated via DPBF as a chemical trapping agent. ROS dominated by ‧OH in BP@CQDs could oxidize DPBF into a colorless product, leading to a decrease in its UV absorption peak at 410 nm and reflecting the ROS concentration from the side [34]. As shown in Fig. S7, the absorption intensity of DPBF displayed a linear decrease in 20 min under NIR irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) in the presence of BP@CQDs, indicating the ROS is being produced continuously. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was performed to confirm further the type of ROS generated by BP@CQDs, and the spin trap 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was employed to monitor the production of ‧OH (Fig. 2D). When the BP@CQDs were in the dark, a silent signal was observed. In comparison, the characteristic 1:2:2:1 quadruplex EPR signal of the stable DMPO/‧OH was detected after the CQDs and BP@CQDs illuminated in an oxygen-saturated DMPO-trapped solution [35]. Moreover, the photodynamic efficacy of BP@CQDs in vitro was detected in Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC12600) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, CVCC216) with 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). Compared with the control, NIR or BP@CQDs groups, BP@CQDs plus NIR group showed evident higher green fluorescence, suggesting BP@CQDs can effectively enhance the intracellular oxidative stress under NIR irradiation (Fig. 2E). Hence, the photothermal and photodynamic response mediated by NIR irradiation makes BP@CQDs a synergistic antibacterial nanoplatform.

Biocompatibility is an important index for biomedical nanomaterials, tested by incubating BP@CQDs with the RBCs and L929 cells [36]. As shown in Fig. 3 A, normal circular morphology of RBCs was observed in BP@CQDs treatment groups, even at concentrations as high as 100 μg/mL, which was similar to the PBS incubating group. Moreover, hemolysis examination was employed to evaluate the hemocompatibility of BP@CQDs, while water and PBS were set as a positive and negative control group, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B, BP@CQDs displayed a low hemolysis ratio at the highest concentration of 100 μg/mL (2.8%), which is still lower than the security value (3.0%) and demonstrates its good hemocompatibility [37]. The BP@CQDs were also conducted with calcein-AM (green) staining live cells and PI (red) staining dead cells to investigate their cytocompatibility. The L929 cells incubated with various concentrations of BP@CQDs solution demonstrated the majority of cells in green and few cells in red because BP and CQDs have been both proved to possess almost negligible cytotoxicity (Fig. 3C). As a result, the cell viability of L929 cells incubated with the increasing concentrations samples in Fig. 3D was close to 100% even if the maximum concentration reached 100 μg/mL, suggesting BP@CQDs has low cytotoxicity to fibroblast cells. All these results demonstrate that BP@CQDs have excellent biocompatibility and can be used as an antibacterial agent.

Encouraged by BP@CQDs' photothermal and photodynamic performance of BP@CQDs, we estimated its in vitro antibacterial activity directly using the plate count method, while E. coli and S. aureus were selected as the model of gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria. As deposited in Fig. 4 A, BP@CQDs without NIR laser irradiation exhibited a slight antibacterial effect against E. coli and S. aureus compared with the control group, which might be attributed to the effect of positive charge from Te-doped CQDs on bacterial reproduction. Upon irradiation with a low power density NIR laser (808 nm, 0.1 W/cm2), viable bacteria were a little less than the pure material group, suggesting a slight photothermal process and photodynamic is at work. Notably, an apparent bacteria-killing phenomenon can be observed when the laser power was increased to 1.5 W/cm2, and the S. aureus was almost entirely killed. In addition, the antibacterial activity was further evaluated by quantitative analysis in Fig. 4B. After E. coli was treated with BP@CQDs, BP@CQDs plus 0.1 W/cm2 NIR, and BP@CQDs plus 1.5 W/cm2 NIR, the bacterial survival rates were 92.7%, 81.6%, and 8.3%, respectively. Expectantly, the S. aureus bacterial survival rates were smaller than that of E. coli, which reached 91.3%, 60.7%, and 1.6%, respectively. These results indicate that BP@CQDs possess outstanding synergistic PDT/PTT antibacterial activity under NIR irradiation. S. aureus biofilms stained with SYTO 9 were exposed to different groups and imaged using CLSM to evaluate their penetrative effect. As shown in Fig. S8, the thickness of PBS was the maximum with an average thickness of around 20.2 μm. The thickness of biofilm in BP@CQDs was 16.2 μm, shown that BP@CQDs alone have an effect on the growth of bacterial biofilms. Subsequently, 0.1 W/cm2 light was applied and a more pronounced damaging effect on the bacterial biofilm was seen. In group BP@CQDs + 1.5 W/cm2, a complete and thick biofilm was not observed. Only scattered colonies of bacteria were seen, suggesting a significant inhibitory effect. This showed that the nanomaterials could significantly inhibit the growth of bacterial biofilms under light and had a strong bactericidal effect.

Based on the commendable photothermal/photodynamic capabilities on S. aureus and E. coli in vitro and negligible cytotoxicity of BP@CQDs, their bacteria inhibition effect in vivo was examined on an S. aureus-infected BALB/c mice as a wound infection model. A comprehensive experiment was performed on all mice to simulate a trauma infection by cutting a wound on the back of mice with surgical scissors and injecting S. aureus cells (1 × 107 CFU) on the fresh wound. Afterwards, the mice were infected for 1 day to form an initial abscess and divided into 4 groups (n = 3) randomly including: (i) PBS as control; (ii) PBS plus NIR (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2); (iii) BP@CQDs plus NIR (808 nm, 0.1 W/cm2); (iv) BP@CQDs plus NIR (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2). A thermal imaging camera observed the temperature changes of the trauma site. As shown in Fig. 4D, E, the damaged area of the PBS group showed almost no temperature rise after 5 min irradiation due to the poor NIR absorption of water. Meanwhile, the wound temperature of the BP@CQDs plus 808 nm laser groups increased quickly from 24.5 °C to 31.8 °C and 50.1 °C, respectively, demonstrating that BP@CQDs still have outstanding photothermal conversion efficacy in vivo. Most importantly, the highest temperature of 50.1 °C that BP@CQDs could reach was higher than the denaturation temperature of bacterial enzymes [38], which confirmed that BP@CQDs had excellent PTT performance in the antibacterial field.

The detailed therapeutic effect of all groups at different time points was also tested, and the photographs of traumas were recorded on days 0, 3, 7, 14 (Fig. 5 A). On day 0, each group exhibited similar wound conditions just after the construction of wound surgery. Compared with the slight scar in group iv, the serious inflammatory responses with obvious ichor were observed after bacterial infection and treatment on day 3. After treating for 7 days, the wound area of BP@CQDs plus high power density NIR group decreased significantly while the other three groups were still edematous and erythematous owing to the infection. At the end of the treatment on the 14th, the trauma of the fourth group was almost completely healed and covered with new skin, while the remaining three groups were in the process of scabbing [39–41]. Subsequently, a corresponding quantitative wound area was performed. As shown in Fig. 5B, the wound size in groups i, ii, iii was more significant than 60% at the end of the first week with the area of 73%, 68%, and 61%, respectively, while group iv has been lower than 50%. Notably, the wound size of BP@CQDs plus high power density NIR group was as small as 16% on day 14, proving the wound is healed. H&E staining of skins was conducted to test further the therapeutic capability of BP@CQDs (Fig. 5D). It was observed that more scar tissues and cell vacuolization appeared in the epithelial layer in groups i, ii, iii. As for BP@CQDs plus high power density NIR group, the inflammatory cells and vacuolization were remarkably reduced, and a well-organized stratified normal epithelial tissue was observed, demonstrating that the synergistic PTT/PDT treatment of BP@CQDs could prevent bacterial invention and accelerate the epithelial tissue formation. In addition, considering the biotoxicity of nanomaterials in vivo, the body weights and H&E staining of major organs of mice during the treatment were recorded. As shown in Figs. 5C and S9, no appreciable difference can be seen in the body weights and major organs, including heart, liver, lungs, spleen, and kidneys, which implies that BP@CQDs is a safe antibacterial agent for wound repair.

In conclusion, we successfully structured an antibacterial nanoplatform with PTT and PDT therapeutic effect by electrostatic interaction, consisting of BP nanosheets and Te-doped CQDs. Combined with the photothermal conversion capability of BP in the NIR region and ‧OH production properties of Te-doped CQDs mediated by NIR, the prepared BP@CQDs could undertake PTT and PDT under a single wavelength. In vitro and in vivo study, BP@CQDs displayed a high-antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli by synergistic PTT/PDT therapeutic effect and good biocompatibility to RBCs, fibroblast, and major organs. As a result, BP@CQDs antibacterial agent could efficiently promote wound healing by preventing bacterial infection. This study provides a great guiding significance for constructing advanced antibiotic substitutes with combined therapeutic effects in the infected wounds and cutaneous skin reconstruction field.

Baolei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Yutian Su: Supervision, Software, Investigation. Shishan Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jian Shen: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52178219).
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2022.102905.

A. Forge, J. Schacht, Aminoglycoside antibiotics, Audiol. Neurotol.5 (2000) 3–22.
